<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Cohesion 1.0 and coupling 0.0 reflect exemplary isolation — this zone demonstrates the ideal structure for an external API client: one production module, collocated tests, no outward dependencies.
[pattern] [warning] The store-contract.test.ts file encodes the shared adapter interface contract but is permanently anchored to the notion-integration-client zone by co-location; future adapters cannot inherit this contract without crossing a zone boundary, creating a scalability constraint on the adapter plugin system.
[anti-pattern] [warning] store-contract.test.ts validates the adapter interface defined in src/store/contracts.ts (integration-adapter-layer zone) — the contract definition and its compliance test live in separate zones, meaning a breaking change to the interface can be made without the zone health tooling detecting that the test zone is affected
[suggestion] [info] Document explicitly that notion-client.ts's zero outbound imports is a design constraint (no PRD schema dependencies in the HTTP client layer) — without documentation this constraint will be silently violated when a developer adds schema-type mapping directly to the client file
[suggestion] [info] Rename zone to 'remote-api-clients' to reflect its role as the vendor implementation layer rather than anchoring to a specific vendor name — future Jira and Linear clients should be co-located here, not in a separate zone

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling indicate this is the cleanest zone in the package — notion-client.ts and its tests are self-contained and make no assumptions about the broader store abstraction
- The store-contract test (store-contract.test.ts) validates the adapter interface rather than Notion-specific behavior, which is valuable; ensure it is parameterized so future adapters (Jira client) can reuse the same contract suite
- notion-client.ts is the sole production file here; if a Jira client is added later, consider whether both clients should share this zone under a 'Remote API Clients' umbrella or remain in the broader integration adapter layer
- Cohesion 1.0 and coupling 0.0 reflect exemplary isolation — this zone demonstrates the ideal structure for an external API client: one production module, collocated tests, no outward dependencies.
- store-contract.test.ts is a generic adapter-contract harness that happens to live in a vendor-named zone; if a Jira or Linear client is added, this contract test will be unreachable from the new adapter zone without either duplicating it or importing across a zone boundary.
- The store-contract.test.ts file encodes the shared adapter interface contract but is permanently anchored to the notion-integration-client zone by co-location; future adapters cannot inherit this contract without crossing a zone boundary, creating a scalability constraint on the adapter plugin system.
- store-contract.test.ts imports the adapter interface to validate it, but the adapter interface itself (contracts.ts) lives in src/store/ within the integration-adapter-layer zone — the contract definition and contract test are in different zones, creating an orphaned test-to-interface relationship that will break if the interface moves
- The zone achieves cohesion 1.0 with zero production imports of its own because notion-client.ts has no runtime dependencies on other package modules — but this isolation is fragile: if notion-client.ts needs access to PRD schema types in the future, the first import will immediately break the metric without signaling that the zone boundary itself changed
- store-contract.test.ts validates the adapter interface defined in src/store/contracts.ts (integration-adapter-layer zone) — the contract definition and its compliance test live in separate zones, meaning a breaking change to the interface can be made without the zone health tooling detecting that the test zone is affected
- Zone name 'notion-integration-client' implies Notion is the canonical integration, but the package is designed as a plugin system (adapter registry) — the zone name anchors a vendor-specific identity as the primary reference point when 'remote-api-client' or 'adapter-client-implementation' would generalize correctly as the adapter pattern scales
- notion-client.ts achieves zero outbound production imports by not referencing any PRD schema types — but the Notion API payloads almost certainly contain date/status fields that will eventually need mapping to rex ItemStatus or PRD schema types; the zero-coupling score is fragile and will break on the first schema-mapping feature
- Rename zone to 'remote-api-clients' to reflect its role as the vendor implementation layer rather than anchoring to a specific vendor name — future Jira and Linear clients should be co-located here, not in a separate zone
- Document explicitly that notion-client.ts's zero outbound imports is a design constraint (no PRD schema dependencies in the HTTP client layer) — without documentation this constraint will be silently violated when a developer adds schema-type mapping directly to the client file
- [call graph] 23 internal calls, 7 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 0.77, coupling: 0.23)

</insights>
